meta.title: 3.8 Checkpoint
meta.description: Complete a multi-step task using at least 4 different slash commands and demonstrate dashboard knowledge.

blocks.content:
## Level 3 Checkpoint

This checkpoint tests whether you know the dashboard — the tools, commands, and modes that make Claude Code work. You will answer knowledge questions and complete a hands-on exercise that uses multiple features together. Answer without looking back at the modules.

blocks.question: Name the six built-in tools Claude Code uses. For each one, give a one-sentence description of when Claude reaches for it.
blocks.answer: **Read** — when Claude needs to see what is in a file (documents, code, images, PDFs). **Edit** — when Claude needs to make targeted changes to an existing file without rewriting the whole thing. **Write** — when Claude needs to create a new file or completely rewrite an existing one. **Glob** — when Claude needs to find files by name pattern (like 'all CSV files'). **Grep** — when Claude needs to search inside files for specific text or patterns. **Bash** — when Claude needs to run a terminal command for anything else (scripts, installs, system info).

blocks.question: You are 25 minutes into a complex session. Claude is giving shorter, less detailed responses and seems to be forgetting earlier context. What is happening and what are your two best options?
blocks.answer: The **context window is filling up**. As the conversation grows, earlier messages are compressed or dropped to make room. Your two best options: (1) Run **`/compact`** to summarize the conversation and free up space — this preserves key information while reducing token usage. (2) Run **`/clear`** and start a fresh session with a well-crafted briefing that includes the essential context — this gives Claude maximum space to work. Use /compact if you want to keep working on the same task. Use /clear if the task can be re-briefed quickly.

blocks.question: Explain the three main permission modes and give a scenario where each is the best choice.
blocks.answer: **Default Mode** (review everything): Best for sensitive work. Scenario: you are editing an important configuration file and want to approve each change individually. **Auto-Accept Edits** (trust file changes, review commands): Best for productive flow. Scenario: Claude is making many small edits to a document and you want it to work quickly, reviewing with /diff at the end. **Plan Mode** (think but do not act): Best for high-stakes planning. Scenario: you want Claude to analyze a complex reorganization and propose a plan before any files are moved. Switch between them with Shift+Tab.

blocks.question: Match each task to the best input method: (A) Analyzing a specific report file. (B) Getting a quick answer about text on your clipboard. (C) Working with files from two different project folders. (D) Applying the same formatting rules to every task.
blocks.answer: **(A) @-mention** — reference the file directly with @reports/file.md. **(B) Piping from clipboard** — `pbpaste | claude -p "your question"` for a quick one-shot answer. **(C) /add-dir** — add the second project folder so Claude can access both. **(D) --system-prompt-file** — put the formatting rules in a file and load it with every session. Each input method has a natural use case, and picking the right one saves time.

blocks.title: Complete this multi-step exercise using at least 4 different slash commands or features. Task: (1) Start a Claude Code session and check your context usage (/context). (2) Ask Claude to create a short document — a meeting agenda, a project outline, or a to-do list. (3) Review the changes (/diff). (4) If you want to modify the result, ask Claude to adjust it. (5) Copy the final result (/copy). (6) Rename the session (/rename). Write down which commands you used and how each one helped.
blocks.solution: A successful completion uses at least: **/context** (awareness of your budget), **/diff** (reviewing what Claude created), **/copy** (getting the output ready to use), and **/rename** (organizing your session). Bonus points for also using **/compact** (if the session is getting long), **/rewind** (if you want to undo and retry), or **Shift+Tab** to switch permission modes. The goal is not just using the commands but understanding when each one adds value. If you found yourself reaching for a command naturally, the dashboard knowledge has landed.

blocks.title: Design a workflow for a task you do regularly. Write out the steps using Claude Code features: which input method would you use, which permission mode, which slash commands for managing the session, and which output method. Example: 'Weekly status email: pipe last week's notes in, use auto-accept for drafting speed, /copy the result, /rename the session as Weekly Update Mar 10.'
blocks.solution: A good workflow design uses at least three different features and matches each one to a specific need. For example: 'Quarterly report: @mention the data file and last quarter's report as a format reference. Start in Plan Mode to review Claude's approach. Switch to Auto-Accept for execution. /diff to review. Ask Claude to save as reports/q1-2025.md. /rename session as Q1 Report Draft.' The key insight: knowing the dashboard lets you design efficient workflows rather than improvising every time.

blocks.content:
## Ready for Level 4?

If you can name the tools, use the slash commands from memory, switch between permission modes, and design workflows that combine multiple features, you are ready. Level 4 moves from knowing the tools to using them strategically — working with context and memory to tackle complex, multi-step projects.

Start with [Level 4: Context and Memory](/level-4/).
